<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reviewing AppDataTable, ListCaseAuditResponse, and existing list page table patterns to plan the Case history datagrid and Officer decision box layout changes.

The right column needs top padding to match the Identity section header height. This will align the Officer decision box with the Identity box. Checking CSS spacing for app-collapsible-section.

Three approaches are being weighed to align the right column with the Identity card: adding a spacer div, restructuring the grid, or using CSS offsets.

A nested grid is the cleanest approach. The Identity header would sit in its own row above the card. The right column would start with a spacer matching the header height. Then the Identity card and Officer decision would share the same row. This places the decision box at the same level as the Identity card rather than the title.

The collapsible header uses a 16px top margin. A CSS offset class is preferable to mirroring the header structure, which was deemed too fragile.

The two-row grid layout is too large a refactor. Adding padding-top to the right column sidebar is simpler. Now checking the exact header height in CSS.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
AppDataTable suits simple tables. MudDataGrid handles heavy filtering. This distinction will guide the history grid implementation.

Case history will move to the bottom of the left column under Civil Status. It will be a collapsible section that stays closed by default. An AppDataTable will hold the audit log entries.

The Officer decision box will align with the Identity box. A CSS offset will match their vertical levels.

Magic CSS offsets are being replaced by a cleaner grid structure. The Identity card will sit inside its own collapsible section within the left column. This removes the need for manual vertical alignment.

The sidebar container will receive a CSS class with top padding. This offset matches the Identity header block height including the title, summary line, and gap margin.

The header text sits flush with no gap between the title and summary line. A nested grid is now the preferred alignment method. The first row would span the full left column width for the Identity title. The second row would place the Identity card on the left and nest the Officer decision alongside the remaining sidebar content on the right.

Splitting the Identity section or using flex auto margins were rejected as too complex. The current idea is an invisible spacer in the right column that mirrors the Identity header height and gap. This would align the Officer decision box vertically without restructuring the grid.

The invisible spacer approach is too fragile. Measuring the Identity header with CSS variables is also unreliable. The cleanest fix is to restructure the grid so the Identity title and card occupy separate areas. This naturally aligns the Officer decision box with the Identity card.

The grid will use two rows. The first row holds the Identity title and chip. The second row holds the Identity card and the Officer decision. An invisible spacer in the right column will match the Identity header height. This avoids restructuring the grid.

A fixed 5.5rem top padding on the sidebar will offset the Officer decision box to match the Identity card. A dedicated invisible spacer component is also being considered for the large-screen breakpoint.

The invisible spacer will be a responsive div that appears only on large screens. It mirrors the Identity header exactly with a centered stack containing an empty h3, a secondary body2 summary, and a neutral status chip. This ensures the Officer decision box aligns precisely with the Identity card.

Invisible character spacers are too hacky. A fixed minimum height matching the Identity header is preferable. The grid will be restructured with two rows. The first row will span the left column for the Identity header and leave the right column empty. This naturally aligns the Officer decision box with the Identity card.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Both changes are in place:

### 1. Case history — collapsible table at the bottom
- Moved from the right sidebar to the **bottom of the main column**, after Civil Status (and Police verification when shown).
- Wrapped in `AppCollapsibleSection`, **collapsed by default** (`_caseHistoryExpanded` starts as `false`).
- Replaced the timeline with an **`AppDataTable`** showing When, Action, Officer, and Details columns.
- Summary shows the event count (e.g. "5 events") when collapsed.

### 2. Officer decision — aligned with Identity card
- Added an **invisible mirror** of the Identity section header in the sidebar (same title, summary, and status chip).
- A 1rem gap matches the `mt-4` spacing before the Identity card.
- On large screens, the Officer decision box now lines up with the **Identity card**, not the section title.
- On smaller screens the spacer is hidden so the sidebar stacks normally.

Refresh the case detail page to see the updates. If the alignment is slightly off on your screen, say so and we can fine-tune the spacer.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The bug is in `DemoOfficerPersistence.AppendToUri`.

Relative URIs are prefixed with `https://localhost`, dropping the original port. The method then rebuilds the path and appends the query string.

`Navigation.NavigateTo` strips the port when given an absolute URL. The fix is to avoid hardcoding `https://localhost` for relative URIs and instead append the query parameter directly to the relative path. Alternatively, MainLayout should pass relative paths during redirects.

The redirect path is relative, so `AppendToUri` converts it to an absolute URL by prepending `https://localhost`. This strips the original port from the current URI.

The fix parses the existing query string and appends the new parameter directly to the relative path. This avoids converting the URI to an absolute address and prevents the browser from stripping the original port.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

The bug is in `DemoOfficerPersistence.AppendToUri`: relative paths get turned into `https://localhost/...` without the port. Checking for tests and fixing it.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
No tests cover this behavior. I'll fix AppendToUri to handle relative URIs without converting them to https://localhost.

I considered using Blazor's NavigationManager helpers, but since this is a static class, fixing AppendToUri directly is the minimal approach.

The updated method uses `UriKind.RelativeOrAbsolute` to detect absolute versus relative paths. Absolute URIs are parsed with `UriPartial.Path` and `QueryHelpers`. Relative URIs are split manually at the query delimiter.

The method rebuilds the query string by joining escaped key-value pairs with ampersands. It returns the original path unchanged when no query parameters exist. Relative paths are preserved as-is, and absolute URIs retain their ports. The path must start with a slash for relative resolution.

Verified that `GetLeftPart(UriPartial.Path)` preserves the port number on absolute URIs. Relative paths returned by `ResolveUriAfterRoleSwitch` will still resolve correctly through `NavigateTo` with `forceLoad: true`.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Good catch — the bug was in `DemoOfficerPersistence.AppendToUri`.

When you switched roles from a page your new role can't access (e.g. `/registration/cases` → `/registration/new-case`), the redirect path was **relative**. The helper turned that into a hardcoded absolute URL:

```csharp
new Uri($"https://localhost{uri}")
```

That dropped the port, so the browser went to `https://localhost/...` instead of `https://localhost:7275/...`.

The fix keeps relative paths relative (e.g. `/registration/new-case?demoOfficer=...`) and only builds full URLs when the input is already absolute — preserving host and port in that case.

After a refresh, switching roles from an inaccessible page should keep you on the correct port with the `demoOfficer` query param intact.

---

_**User**_

When we load the page http://localhost:5155/registration/new-case 

FirstRegistration is selected by default but we can't click on Create case. 

Also maybe can we improve a bit the design of this page?

---

_**Agent (default)**_

Two issues fixed, plus a layout refresh on the new-case page.

### Create case button
The button was tied to `_formValid`, which MudBlazor only sets **after** validation runs. With "First registration" pre-selected, the form looked valid but `_formValid` stayed `false`, so the button stayed disabled.

It now matches the dialog behavior: enabled whenever not submitting, and validation runs on click.

### Design updates
- **Two-column layout** — form card on the left, "What happens next?" guidance on the right
- **`AppCard`** with icon for the form and a dedicated success state after creation
- **Human-readable visit reasons** via shared `VisitReasonLabels` (also used in the dialog)
- **Contextual description** under the select that updates with the chosen reason
- **Recording officer** shown on the form
- **Workflow steps** with icons explaining reception → population officer → police
- **"Create another case"** action after success, so reception can open multiple files in a row

Refresh `/registration/new-case` to see the changes.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Unassigned cases will be added to the actionable list in GetReviewDashboardHandler.

A statistic tile for unassigned cases may be added. Integration tests could follow. Unassigned cases are those where LockedByOfficerId is null. The status should likely be Intake. Releasing a lock clears the lock owner without removing AssignedOfficerId.

Unassigned cases are those with no assigned or locked officer. A released lock should not count as unassigned.

Only Intake cases will count as unassigned. AwaitingPoliceVerification and similar statuses will be excluded.

Unassigned cases will be prioritized immediately after Ready for approval. They represent new reception work requiring immediate attention.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reviewing RegistrationTestHelpers and RoleBoundaries tests to understand how unassigned cases appear in the review dashboard.

Decided to keep the unassigned check in the handler rather than adding a domain method. The summary text will read "Unassigned — awaiting intake". Items will be sorted by priority: ready for approval, then unassigned, then suspended, then awaiting police. Unassigned cases will be ordered by oldest first.

Settled on a simpler sorting strategy. Ready cases come first. Unassigned cases follow. Suspended cases come last. All other fields will be ordered descending.

A tuple-based SortKey method will replace the messy conditional ThenBy expression. Ready cases will sort by oldest first. Unassigned cases will sort by oldest first. Suspended cases will sort by newest first.

The sort key is being simplified further. A priority integer will rank cases by priority ascending, with unassigned cases ordered oldest-first and all other cases newest-first.

Working out the exact ThenBy expression for the sort key. Using DateTimeOffset.MaxValue minus OpenedAt ticks is invalid. The plan is to branch on IsUnassigned to apply ascending OpenedAt only for unassigned cases, while leaving other priorities descending.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Unassigned cases from reception now show up on the review dashboard.

### Needs my attention
Intake cases with **no assigned officer and no lock** (freshly opened at reception) appear in the actionable table with summary **"Unassigned — awaiting intake"**. They rank below "Ready for decision" but above suspended and police-waiting cases.

### New statistic tile
An **Unassigned** counter was added (highlighted when count > 0), linking to the cases list.

### Also fixed
The review dashboard was failing on SQLite because case listing ordered by `DateTimeOffset` in the database query. Ordering now happens in memory after fetch, which works with SQLite in dev and tests.

To verify: open a case as the reception officer, switch to a population officer, and check the review dashboard — the new case should appear in both the tile and the table.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
MudButton chips inside a MudStack component will provide the one-click filter buttons.

Selected filters will use Filled variant and Primary color; unselected ones will use Outlined. URL query parameters could enable deep linking from the review dashboard, though this was not explicitly requested.

The page will read a `filter` query parameter via `[SupplyParameterFromQuery]`. It will map `unassigned` and `mine` values to their corresponding quick filters, then load the filtered list on initialization.

The review dashboard links will include the new query parameter for deep linking. An empty state will display when no cases match the current filter. The layout places the search bar above the filter row, which uses a horizontal MudStack with wrapped chips.

A private enum and helper methods will manage the quick filters. `IsMyCase` checks if the current officer owns or is assigned to a case. `IsUnassigned` checks for null assigned and locked officer IDs. The `ApplyFilter` method will apply the active selection to the data table.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Quick filters are now on the registration cases list, above the table:

### Filter buttons
- **All cases** — default, shows everything
- **My cases** — locked to you or assigned to you
- **Unassigned** — no assignee and no lock (reception intakes waiting for pickup)

The active filter uses a filled primary style; others stay outlined. Filters work together with the search bar.

### Empty states
When a filter returns no rows, a specific message is shown instead of an empty table.

### Dashboard links
Review dashboard tiles link straight into the filtered list:
- **My open cases** → `/registration/cases?filter=mine`
- **Unassigned** → `/registration/cases?filter=unassigned`

Refresh the cases page to try it out.

Phase **8.1** is documented across the docs set. Main reference:

**[docs/phases/phase-8.1-role-boundaries-case-locking.md](docs/phases/phase-8.1-role-boundaries-case-locking.md)**

The phase is titled **Role boundaries, case locking & officer UX** and covers:

### Access control (original 8.1)
- Claim/release lock, role matrix, authorization guards, audit events, API routes

### Officer UX (this session)
- Reception **new case** page (layout, Create fix, post-create guidance)
- **Review dashboard** unassigned queue + statistic tile
- **Case list** quick filters + `?filter=` deep links
- **Case detail** — history datagrid, officer decision alignment
- **Demo role switching** — port-safe URL persistence

Also updated:
- [docs/phases/README.md](docs/phases/README.md) — phase title
- [docs/ROADMAP.md](docs/ROADMAP.md) — progress table + new Phase 8.1 section
- [docs/features/registration/README.md](docs/features/registration/README.md) — expanded Phase 8.1 feature summary

Each doc includes a demo script, deliverables checklist, key files, tests, and deferred items.
